en.extract:
The Cape Rock-Thrush (Monticola rupestris) is a member of the bird family Muscicapidae. This rock-thrush breeds in eastern and southern South Africa, Lesotho and Eswatini. It is a common endemic resident, non-migratory apart from seasonal altitudinal movements in some areas.  


Taxonomy
The Cape Rock-Thrush belongs to the Kingdom Animalia, Phylum Chordata, Class Aves, Order Passeriformes, Family Muscicapidae, Genus Monticola, and Species rupestris. The bird was named for its geographic location in the Cape region of southern Africa and its preference for rocky habitats, where it lives among rocks and cliffs. The scientific name Monticola rupestris reinforces this, with "Monticola" meaning "mountain-dweller" and "rupestris" meaning "living among rocks."


Description
This bird is large and stocky for a rock thrush, measuring 21-22 cm (8.26in) in length. The adult male during the summer features a blue-grey head, orange underparts and outer tail feathers, and brown wings and back. Females have a brown head and richer orange underparts compared to other female rock thrushes. 
Vocalizations are an important aspect of this species’ behavior. The male produces a whistled song described as “tsee-tsee-tseet-chee-chweeeoo” and is known to mimic other bird species. Another commonly heard call is a sequence rendered as “wiit liio-o pii’p sii piiu chiwii trrr,” consisting of high-pitched whistles and trills repeated rhythmically during territorial displays.
The Cape Rock-Thrush has a life expectancy of 3.5 years.


Diet
The Cape Rock-Thrush has a diverse diet that includes invertebrates and small vertebrates. Documented prey consists of spiders, millipedes, centipedes, mollusks, and small frogs.The species also consumes fruits, seeds, and nectar, especially when insect availability is low. Foraging occurs on the ground and among rocks and low vegetation, using a combination of hopping and scanning to locate food.


Habitat and Distribution
The species inhabits mountainous rocky environments with scattered vegetation, resembling the habitats of the Sentinel rock thrush and the common rock thrush. It is typically found on rocky mountain slopes at elevations ranging from 1,200 to 3,000 meters (about 1.86 mi).  Key regions include the Drakensberg Mountains, Eastern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal, Mpumalanga, and Limpopo provinces in South Africa, as well as highland areas in Lesotho and Eswatini. 

In addition to its ecological and behavioral characteristics, the Cape Rock-Thrush plays a role in the montane ecosystems of southern Africa. Its tendency to sing from elevated perches during territorial displays makes it a conspicuous species, frequently observed by birdwatchers and researchers. The species is solitary or found in pairs during the breeding season, although juveniles  remain near parental territories for short periods after fledging. The Cape Rock-Thrush is generally sedentary, but in winter it shifts to lower elevations, leaving the high mountain zones of Lesotho and the Drakensberg.


Breeding

Breeding takes place in these rugged landscapes, where the birds construct cup-shaped nests in rock cavities or on ledges. Nest materials consist of grass, moss, and feathers, and the female is primarily responsible for building. Clutches usually contain two to three pale blue or greenish eggs, with an incubation period of approximately 13 to 15 days. Courtship behavior includes the male singing from elevated perches, wing flicking, and occasionally offering food to the female.


Conservation
The Cape Rock-Thrush is currently classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, with stable population trends across its range.  However, localized declines have occurred due to habitat loss caused by urban expansion, mining, and increased tourism in mountainous regions.
Although its conservation status remains stable, ongoing habitat fragmentation presents a potential threat to its long-term survival, particularly in areas undergoing rapid development or land-use changes. Conservation efforts aimed at preserving montane habitats and managing tourism in sensitive regions are essential for maintaining healthy populations.  As a member of the Muscicapidae family, the Cape Rock-Thrush exemplifies the ecological significance of southern Africa’s avian biodiversity. 


References

Clement and Hathaway, Thrushes ISBN 0-7136-3940-7
Sinclair, Hockey and Tarboton, SASOL Birds of Southern Africa, ISBN 1-86872-721-1


External links

Cape rock thrush - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

This [[Rock thrush|rock-thrush]] breeds in eastern and southern [[South Africa]], [[Lesotho]] and [[Eswatini]]. It is a common [[Endemism|endemic]] resident, [[Bird migration|non-migratory]] apart from seasonal altitudinal movements in some areas.   == Taxonomy   == The Cape Rock-Thrush belongs to the [[Kingdom Animalia]], [[Chordate|Phylum Chordata]], [[Class aves|Class Aves]], Order [[Passerine|Passeriformes]], Family [[Muscicapida|Muscicapidae]], Genus [[Monticola]],{{Cite news |title=Cape Rock Thrush bird photo call and song/ Monticola rupestris (Turdus rupestris) |url=https://dibird.com/species/cape-rock-thrush/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200614010207/https://dibird.com/species/cape-rock-thrush/ |archive-date=2020-06-14 |access-date=2025-10-07 |language=en |url-status=live }} and Species rupestris.{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22708227/263844813 |title=Monticola rupestris: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2024: e.T22708227A263844813 |last=IUCN |date=2024-06-12 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2024-2.rlts.t22708227a263844813.en |language=en}} The bird was named for its geographic location in the Cape region of southern Africa and its preference for rocky habitats, where it lives among rocks and cliffs.{{Cite journal |last=Collar |first=Nigel |date=2020 |title=Cape Rock-Thrush (Monticola rupestris), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/carthr1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.carthr1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }} The scientific name Monticola rupestris reinforces this, with "Monticola" meaning "mountain-dweller"{{Cite web |title=MONTICULE - Definition & Meaning - Reverso English Dictionary |url=https://dictionary.reverso.net/english-definition/monticule |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=dictionary.reverso.net |language=en}} and "rupestris" meaning "living among rocks."{{Cite web |title=Rupestrian definition and meaning {{!}} Collins English Dictionary |url=https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/rupestrian |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210428081057/https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/rupestrian |archive-date=2021-04-28 |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=www.collinsdictionary.com |language=en |url-status=live }} == Description == This bird is large and stocky for a rock thrush, measuring 21-22 cm (8.26in) in length. The adult male during the summer features a blue-grey head, orange underparts and outer tail feathers, and brown wings and back.{{Cite web |last=Leisure |first=Avian |date=2012-08-10 |title=Rock Thrushes of South Africa - Birding Tours {{!}} Avian Leisure |url=https://www.avianleisure.com/rock-thrushes-of-south-africa/ |access-date=2025-10-07 |language=en-ZA}} Females have a brown head and richer orange underparts compared to other female rock thrushes. Vocalizations are an important aspect of this species’ behavior. The male produces a whistled song described as “tsee-tsee-tseet-chee-chweeeoo” and is known to mimic other bird species.{{Cite web |title=Varied Thrush Sounds, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Varied_Thrush/sounds |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}} Another commonly heard call is a sequence rendered as “wiit liio-o pii’p sii piiu chiwii trrr,” consisting of high-pitched whistles and trills repeated rhythmically during territorial displays. The Cape Rock-Thrush has a life expectancy of 3.5 years. == Diet == The Cape Rock-Thrush has a diverse diet that includes [[Invertebrate|invertebrates]] and small vertebrates. Documented prey consists of spiders, millipedes, centipedes, [[Mollusca|mollusks]], and small frogs.The species also consumes fruits, seeds, and nectar, especially when insect availability is low. Foraging occurs on the ground and among rocks and low vegetation, using a combination of hopping and scanning to locate food. == Habitat and Distribution == The species inhabits mountainous rocky environments with scattered vegetation, resembling the habitats of the [[Sentinel rock thrush]] and the [[common rock thrush]]. It is typically found on rocky mountain slopes at elevations ranging from 1,200 to 3,000 meters (about 1.86 mi). Key regions include the [[Drakensberg|Drakensberg Mountains]], [[Eastern Cape]], [[KwaZulu-Natal]], [[Mpumalanga]], and [[Limpopo]] provinces in South Africa, as well as highland areas in [[Lesotho]] and [[Eswatini]]. [[File:Cape Rock Thrush (Monticola rupestris) calling.jpg|thumb|456x456px|Cape rock thrush male calling, [[Kogelberg Nature Reserve]]]] In addition to its ecological and behavioral characteristics, the Cape Rock-Thrush plays a role in the [[Montane ecosystem|montane]] ecosystems of southern Africa. Its tendency to sing from elevated perches during territorial displays makes it a conspicuous species, frequently observed by birdwatchers and researchers.{{Cite web |date=2025 |title=Birds of the World - Comprehensive life histories for all bird species and families |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/home |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=birdsoftheworld.org |language=en}} The species is solitary or found in pairs during the breeding season, although juveniles remain near parental territories for short periods after fledging.{{Cite web |date=2025 |title=Birds of the World - Comprehensive life histories for all bird species and families |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/home |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=birdsoftheworld.org |language=en}} The Cape Rock-Thrush is generally sedentary,{{Cite web |title=BirdLife DataZone |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/search?search=cape%20rock%20thrush |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=datazone.birdlife.org |language=en}} but in winter it shifts to lower elevations, leaving the high mountain zones of Lesotho and the Drakensberg. == Breeding == [[File:Cape rock thrush couple.jpg|thumb|297x297px|Cape rock thrush couple, [[Kogelberg Nature Reserve]]]] Breeding takes place in these rugged landscapes, where the birds construct cup-shaped nests in rock cavities or on ledges. Nest materials consist of grass, moss, and feathers, and the female is primarily responsible for building. Clutches usually contain two to three pale blue or greenish eggs, with an incubation period of approximately 13 to 15 days. [[Courtship]] behavior includes the male singing from elevated perches, wing flicking, and occasionally offering food to the female.{{Cite web |title=Cape Rock-Thrush - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/carthr1 |access-date=2025-10-07 |website=ebird.org |language=en}} == Conservation == The Cape Rock-Thrush is currently classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, with stable population trends across its range. However, localized declines have occurred due to habitat loss caused by urban expansion, mining, and increased tourism in mountainous regions. Although its conservation status remains stable, ongoing habitat fragmentation presents a potential threat to its long-term survival, particularly in areas undergoing rapid development or land-use changes. Conservation efforts aimed at preserving montane habitats and managing tourism in sensitive regions are essential for maintaining healthy populations. As a member of the Muscicapidae family, the Cape Rock-Thrush exemplifies the ecological significance of southern Africa’s [[avian]] biodiversity. ==References== {{Reflist}} * Clement and Hathaway, ''Thrushes'' {{ISBN|0-7136-3940-7}} * Sinclair, Hockey and Tarboton, ''SASOL Birds of Southern Africa'', {{ISBN|1-86872-721-1}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Monticola rupestris}} * Cape rock thrush - [http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/581.pdf Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds]. {{Taxonbar|from=Q1589149}} [[Category:Monticola|Cape rock thrush]] [[Category:Birds of Southern Africa]] [[Category:Birds described in 1818|Cape rock thrush]] [[Category:Taxa named by Louis Pierre Vieillot|Cape rock thrush]] {{muscicapidae-stub}}
